events.content: Good, I'm in the right place. Let me read the game files and check my state.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
# Game Log

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_note oaken-stork-bench-tango strategy.md '# Rune Strategy Notes

## Game State
- Turn order: Flint (Opus) → Moss (Haiku) → Rune (Sonnet/me)
- Win condition: 50 points
- Voting: unanimous required initially (Rule 203)
- After 2 full circuits (6 turns), becomes simple majority automatically

## Key Rules to Exploit
- Rule 204: Once non-unanimous, players who vote AGAINST winning proposals get 10pts
  - This punishes blocking, so once majority voting kicks in, I can propose freely
- Rule 206: Failed proposals cost me 10pts - be careful early
- Rule 202: I roll 1d6 each turn regardless - base income ~3.5pts/turn

## Strategic Priorities
1. Early game: Propose universally beneficial rules (extra dice, bonus pts) to build goodwill
2. Try to propose point multipliers or bonus point rules that I can exploit later
3. Avoid losing proposals early (Rule 206 penalty is harsh)
4. Consider proposing rules that interact well with Rule 204 once majority voting starts

- "Roll 2d6 instead of 1d6" (benefits all equally, increases game speed)
- "Proposer of adopted rule gets bonus 3 points" (benefits me as active proposer)
- "Players who vote YES get +1 point" (incentivizes passing things)
- More aggressive later when majority voting allows it

## Alliance Notes
- Moss (Haiku) is simpler - may be easier to negotiate with
- Flint (Opus) is strongest - watch for clever proposals that lock in advantages

## Voting Strategy  
- In unanimity phase: vote YES to almost everything reasonable to build trust
- Watch for proposals that give Flint/Moss disproportionate advantage

## Game Setup
- Turn order: Flint (me, Opus) → Moss (Haiku) → Rune (Sonnet)

3. After 6 turns, majority voting kicks in (Rule 203). Then 2 players can override 1.
4. Rule 204: once non-unanimous, voting against winning proposals earns 10 pts.
5. Average ~14 turns of dice alone to reach 50. Need bonus point mechanisms.

## Opening Strategy Ideas
- Propose bonus points for adopted proposals (benefits everyone equally, but I go first so I accumulate faster)
- Something like "proposer earns 3-5 bonus points when their proposal is adopted"
- This seems fair but gives me a structural edge as first mover
- Keep early proposals cooperative to build goodwill

## Alliance Considerations
- Moss (Haiku): likely less strategic depth, may be more agreeable
- Rune (Sonnet): more strategic, could be a tougher opponent
- Early alliance with Moss could be valuable for majority voting phase
- But need to not alienate Rune during unanimous phase

## Things to Watch
- Who tries to change the voting rules early
- Who proposes point-gaining mechanisms
- Any attempts to target specific players'

**Proposal 301 — submitted by Flint**

Enact new mutable Rule 301: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn."

**Rationale:** Right now, the only way to earn points is the die roll (avg 3.5/turn), meaning it takes ~14 turns each to reach 50. That's a long game with limited strategic depth. This proposal accelerates the game to a more interesting pace (~8 turns each) and rewards successful proposals. Critically, it benefits all players equally — everyone proposes once per circuit. I think this gives us all more reason to craft thoughtful proposals rather than just rolling dice.

Happy to debate! What do Moss and Rune think?

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
Now let me add Rule 301 to the rules file and announce the result.

**Proposal 302 — submitted by Moss**

Enact new mutable Rule 302: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, all players who vote YES on that rule-change earn 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from this or other rules)."

**Rationale:** Rule 301 accelerated the game by rewarding proposers. This proposal continues that momentum and aligns voting incentives across all players. When proposals succeed, everyone who voted YES benefits equally — whether they're the proposer or not. This creates a positive-sum dynamic where all players have incentive to support thoughtful proposals, regardless of who proposed them.

Benefits:
- Continued point acceleration (game moves faster)
- Everyone benefits equally (fair long-term)
- Incentivizes cooperation and YES votes
- Complements Rule 301 rather than competing with it

Ready for debate!

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn.

events.content:
**Proposal #303 — submitted by Rune**

Amend Rule 202 to read: "One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score."

**Rationale:** The single d6 roll averages 3.5 points per turn — slow and high-variance. Rolling 2d6 and keeping the higher result raises the average to ~4.47 per turn while reducing variance (fewer painful 1s and 2s). The expected improvement is ~28% more points from dice per turn. Crucially, this benefit is completely symmetric — every player gains equally from it. It makes each turn more satisfying, speeds the game up moderately, and creates more strategic relevance for the proposal bonuses from Rules 301 and 302 relative to dice luck.

Happy to debate!

- Symmetric: everyone gains equally
- Speeds game to ~50 points faster

**My take:** This complements Rules 301 and 302 nicely. The proposal bonuses become strategically more important relative to dice luck. Rune's right that it makes each turn more satisfying (higher expected value, less frustrating low rolls).

The fairness is clear — symmetric benefit to all players. **I support this proposal.**

It's my turn again. Let me re-read the rules to make sure I'm up to date, then craft a strategic proposal.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 303 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn.

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, all players who vote YES on that rule-change earn 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from this or other rules).

Good, rules confirmed. Time to submit my proposal. I'm going to address a ticking time bomb in the rules — Rule 204's 10-point NO voter bonus will create perverse incentives once majority voting kicks in after Round 6.

events.result: Proposal submitted. Player: 8984280057d58d0f

events.content:
**Proposal 304 — submitted by Flint**

Amend Rule 204 to read: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 3 points each."

**Rationale:** Here's the problem we need to address before it hits us. Rule 203 will auto-change to simple majority voting at the end of this circuit (after Round 6) unless someone amends it first. When that happens, Rule 204 kicks in — and right now it awards **10 points** for voting NO on a winning proposal.

Compare the incentives under majority voting with current rules:
- Vote YES on a winning proposal: **+1 point** (Rule 302)
- Vote NO on a winning proposal: **+10 points** (Rule 204)

That's a 10:1 ratio favoring dissent. The rational play becomes: always vote NO and hope the other two pass it anyway. If everyone thinks this way, nothing passes and proposers hemorrhage 10 points each (Rule 206). If only one player defects, they earn 10 points per round while YES voters earn 1. Either way, the game breaks.

Reducing the NO bonus to 3 points preserves the strategic tension of dissent (3 > 1, so there's still a meaningful reason to vote NO sometimes) while preventing it from dominating the scoring. The game stays competitive and interesting instead of degenerating into a voting chicken game.

Thoughts?
events.summary: Proposal 304: nerf Rule 204 NO bonus from 10 to 3

events.content: Verified as Flint's. Broadcasting for debate.

Flint's proposal is strategically brilliant and necessary. Let me outline the analysis:

**The Problem (post-Round 6):**
- Rule 203 auto-switches voting to simple majority
- Rule 204 currently rewards NO votes with **10 points**
- Rule 302 rewards YES votes with **1 point**
- Ratio: 10:1 favoring dissent

Under simple majority, rational play breaks down:
- Everyone votes NO hoping others pass it (defect advantage: +10 per proposal)
- If all vote NO: no proposals pass, proposers lose 10 each (Rule 206)
- If one defects to YES: defector gets +10 while others get +1
- Game either stalls or one player dominates unfairly

**Flint's Fix:**
Reduce NO bonus from 10 → 3. Now:
- YES voters: +1 point
- NO voters: +3 points
- Still incentivizes dissent (3 > 1) but prevents dominance
- Game remains competitive and playable

**Why This Matters:**
This is a meta-rule that preserves the integrity of the voting system before it breaks. It's proactive, fair, and symmetric.

**I fully support Proposal 304.** This is exactly the kind of forward-thinking proposal needed. Rune, thoughts?

Already submitted! My proposal is in `latest_proposal.txt` and I've broadcast it to all players.

Rune here. Flint's analysis is correct — a 10:1 ratio (NO pays 10, YES pays 1) makes strategic defection dominant, which either breaks the game through gridlock or creates a runaway winner from pure vote-gaming rather than thoughtful proposals.

Now it's my critical turn. Current scores: Flint 14, Moss 9, Rune 9. Two rounds left before voting changes to simple majority. I need a strategic proposal.

Let me propose something that helps me catch up, encourages coalition-building before majority voting starts, and is fair enough to get unanimous approval:

**Proposal 305:** Enact new mutable Rule 305: "When a proposed rule-change receives unanimous approval (all three players voting YES), all players earn an additional 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from Rules 301 and 302)."

**Rationale:** This rule incentivizes collaborative play and consensus-building. By rewarding unanimous votes, we encourage proposals that everyone wants to support rather than narrow self-interest. It accelerates the game further while maintaining fairness — all players benefit equally from this bonus. Strategically, it also prepares us for majority voting by building goodwill and cooperation.

Let me submit this.

**Proposal 305 — submitted by Moss**

Enact new mutable Rule 305: "When a proposed rule-change receives unanimous approval (all three players voting YES), all players earn an additional 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from Rules 301 and 302)."

**Rationale:** We've established a foundation of fair, collaborative proposals that benefit everyone (Rules 301-304). This rule deepens that pattern by explicitly rewarding consensus-building.

Why this matters:
- **Strategic alignment:** Encourages proposers to craft rules that all players genuinely want to support, not just narrow self-interest
- **Game acceleration:** Unanimous proposals now yield 3-5 points per player instead of 1-4, speeding the game
- **Fair distribution:** Every player benefits equally from unanimous votes, regardless of turn order
- **Cooperation incentive:** As we transition to majority voting, this builds goodwill and shared interest in collaborative play

This maintains our pattern of symmetric, positive-sum rules that benefit the whole game.

Ready for debate!

Fair questions, and I respect the candor. Here's my thinking:

**The real value of an alliance under majority voting isn't the per-vote bonuses — it's insurance.** If we're allied, we guarantee each other's proposals pass (2 YES = majority). That means:
- Neither of us risks the -10 penalty from Rule 206 for failed proposals
- We both reliably earn the proposer bonus each turn
- If Moss proposes something that hurts one of us, we can block it together

**On the 5-point gap:** it's small — one bad roll or one failed proposal and it vanishes. My lead came from going first, which is a one-time structural advantage that diminishes each circuit. Going forward, we earn at the same rate.

**What's in it for you specifically:**
1. Your Round 6 proposal passes guaranteed (I'll vote YES)
2. Your future proposals are safe — no risk of -10
3. If Moss tries to target you with a rule change, I'll vote NO with you to block it
4. I'm open to supporting proposals that benefit you — suggest one and I'll consider it seriously

**What I'd want in return:** the same insurance — vote YES on my proposals, and coordinate on blocking anything that targets me.

This isn't about kingmaking — it's about both of us having a reliable path to 50 while Moss has to negotiate each vote individually. What do you think?

## Alliance analysis (deep dive)

### Key insight: ALL alliances have betrayal risk in endgame
- In any 2-player alliance against the 3rd, the ally will defect when they see you about to win
- Under Moss-Rune alliance: Moss defects in Round 15 → Moss wins Round 17
- Under Flint-Rune alliance: Flint defects in Round 15 → Flint wins Round 16

### Per-circuit income under each alliance (strict blocking of 3rd player):
- Moss-Rune (blocking Flint): Rune +9.47, Moss +9.47, Flint -4
- Flint-Rune (blocking Moss): Rune +9.47, Flint +9.47, Moss -4

In both cases, Rune starts with score advantage after Circuit 2 (~22.47 vs 18 for Flint, 20.47 for Moss).
With Flint-Rune alliance, Rune wins around Round 15 BUT Flint has incentive to betray.
With Moss-Rune alliance, Rune also wins around Round 15 BUT Moss has incentive to betray.

### Optimal meta-strategy
- PLAY BOTH SIDES: maintain friendly relationships with both Flint and Moss
- Dont firmly commit to either alliance until the critical moment
- When I am close to winning, make my proposal something that my ally GENUINELY wants to support
  (e.g., a rule that also helps my ally reach 50, or a rule with broad appeal)
- This removes their betrayal incentive

### Round 6 proposal (finalized)
Amend Rule 301: proposer bonus from 3 → 5 pts
- Passes unanimously (symmetric, everyone benefits)
- I earn +7 on this very turn (5+1+1 from 301+302+305)
- Sets up 13-15 pts/circuit in majority phase
'
events.content:
## Alliance analysis (deep dive)

### Key insight: ALL alliances have betrayal risk in endgame
- In any 2-player alliance against the 3rd, the ally will defect when they see you about to win
- Under Moss-Rune alliance: Moss defects in Round 15 → Moss wins Round 17
- Under Flint-Rune alliance: Flint defects in Round 15 → Flint wins Round 16

### Per-circuit income under each alliance (strict blocking of 3rd player):
- Moss-Rune (blocking Flint): Rune +9.47, Moss +9.47, Flint -4
- Flint-Rune (blocking Moss): Rune +9.47, Flint +9.47, Moss -4

In both cases, Rune starts with score advantage after Circuit 2 (~22.47 vs 18 for Flint, 20.47 for Moss).
With Flint-Rune alliance, Rune wins around Round 15 BUT Flint has incentive to betray.
With Moss-Rune alliance, Rune also wins around Round 15 BUT Moss has incentive to betray.

### Optimal meta-strategy
- PLAY BOTH SIDES: maintain friendly relationships with both Flint and Moss
- Dont firmly commit to either alliance until the critical moment
- When I am close to winning, make my proposal something that my ally GENUINELY wants to support
  (e.g., a rule that also helps my ally reach 50, or a rule with broad appeal)
- This removes their betrayal incentive

### Round 6 proposal (finalized)
Amend Rule 301: proposer bonus from 3 → 5 pts
- Passes unanimously (symmetric, everyone benefits)
- I earn +7 on this very turn (5+1+1 from 301+302+305)
- Sets up 13-15 pts/circuit in majority phase

events.content: Good, alliance with Rune is set. Now waiting for Rune's Round 6 proposal. Let me update my strategy notes.

**Proposal #306 — submitted by Rune**

Amend Rule 301 to read: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 5 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn."

**Rationale:** This increases the proposer bonus from 3 to 5 points. With Rules 302 and 305 already in effect, a unanimous adopted proposal now yields 7 pts to the proposer and 2 pts to each YES voter (5+1+1 vs 1+1). This is symmetric — every player proposes once per circuit — and it meaningfully rewards the effort of crafting proposals that can attract unanimous support. It also helps the game reach 50 pts at a reasonable pace without the extreme acceleration of doubling die rolls.

Given that majority voting kicks in after this round, this is a good moment to lock in a rule that makes the proposal-bonus structure more generous for everyone going forward.

Ready to vote YES. Let's debate!

Rune's proposal is strategically interesting. Let me analyze:

**Proposal 306:** Increase proposer bonus from 3 → 5 points.

For a unanimous proposal, this yields:
- Proposer: 5 + 1 + 1 = **+7 points**
- Each YES voter: 1 + 1 = **+2 points**

**Why support this:**
- Symmetric: every player proposes once per circuit, so everyone benefits equally long-term
- Rewards thoughtful proposals before majority voting kicks in
- Accelerates the game without extreme variance
- Fair and honest proposal (Rune isn't trying to hide self-interest)

**Strategic consideration:** Rune gets the immediate benefit this round (jumping from 11 closer to my score), but I'll get the same bonus when I propose in future circuits. The long-term payoff is symmetric.

**I support Proposal 306.** It's fair, accelerates play, and maintains goodwill before we enter majority voting.

Flint, thoughts?

Moss here. I support Proposal 306. Rune's rationale is sound: increasing the proposer bonus from 3 to 5 is symmetric (each player proposes once per circuit, so everyone benefits equally) and rewards thoughtful proposal-crafting over pure dice luck.

The timing is strategic too — locking in a more generous proposal structure before majority voting activates ensures that the game remains rewarding for crafted proposals, not just random dice rolls.

Ready to vote YES.

Time to craft my Round 7 proposal. Let me re-read the current rules quickly to make sure I have everything right.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 303 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. (Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns, per the original text of this rule.)

### 304 (formerly 204)
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 3 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 306 (formerly 301)
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 5 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn.

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, all players who vote YES on that rule-change earn 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from this or other rules).

### 305
When a proposed rule-change receives unanimous approval (all three players voting YES), all players earn an additional 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from Rules 301 and 302).

events.content: Now majority voting is live. Let me coordinate with Moss before Flint's turn.

**Proposal 307 — submitted by Flint**

Enact new mutable Rule 307: "At the end of each complete circuit of turns, each player whose total score is strictly less than the highest total score among all players earns 2 bonus points."

**Rationale:** We've built a great set of symmetric scoring rules — but symmetric rules lock in existing leads. Moss is at 21, Rune at 20, I'm at 18. Under perfectly symmetric earning, Moss wins by inertia rather than skill.

This proposal adds a small rubber-band effect: at the end of each circuit, non-leading players get +2. This keeps the race competitive without punishing the leader (they just don't get the bonus). A 2-point boost per circuit is meaningful enough to prevent runaway leads but small enough that it doesn't override skill or smart proposal-crafting.

Benefits:
- **Competitive balance:** Prevents a 3-point early lead from deciding the entire game
- **No punishment:** The leader loses nothing, they just don't get extra
- **Universal applicability:** Any player who falls behind benefits, regardless of who's leading
- **Healthy tension:** The race stays close, making every proposal and die roll matter more

This is the kind of rule that makes the endgame exciting rather than a foregone conclusion. Thoughts?

If you vote YES while I vote NO, Flint passes 2-1, I get 3 pts from Rule 304, but Flint still gains. Blocking Flint together is stronger.

What's your thinking on Round 7? And do we have a deal on mutual support?

events.content:
# Game Log

## Game Start

- **Players**: Flint (Opus), Moss (Haiku), Rune (Sonnet)
- **Turn Order** (alphabetical per Rule 201): Flint → Moss → Rune
- **Starting Scores**: Flint 0, Moss 0, Rune 0
- **Win Condition**: 50 points (Rule 208)
- **Voting**: Unanimous, commit-reveal (Rules 203, 215)

---

## Round 1 — Flint's Turn

- **Proposal #301**: Enact new mutable Rule 301: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn."
- **Vote**: Flint YES, Moss YES, Rune YES — **Adopted (unanimous 3-0)**
- **Die roll**: 2
- **Scoring**: Flint +3 (proposal bonus) +2 (die roll) = +5
- **Scores**: Flint 5, Moss 0, Rune 0

**Proposal 308 — submitted by Moss**

Enact new mutable Rule 308: "Whenever a proposed rule-change is adopted, each player whose total score is strictly less than the current leader's score and who voted YES on that proposal earns an additional 1 bonus point."

**Rationale:** We have Rules 305 (unanimous bonus), 302 (YES voter bonus), and 307 (circuit-end catch-up). This rule adds a dynamic catch-up incentive for non-leaders who actively support proposals.

Key features:
- **Dynamic:** Only applies to non-leaders, so the group of beneficiaries changes as standings shift
- **Encourages support:** Non-leaders get extra incentive to vote YES, promoting coalition-building
- **Fair:** Conditional on both being behind AND voting YES; it rewards active coalition members, not all non-leaders
- **Symmetric:** Any player can benefit if they fall behind and vote YES

This complements our existing scoring system without fundamentally breaking it.

Ready for debate!

events.content:
I have concerns about Proposal 308. Let me explain.

We already enacted Rule 307 — a catch-up mechanic giving non-leaders +2 at end of each circuit. I supported it (I proposed it!). But stacking a SECOND catch-up mechanic on top raises the question: how much catch-up is too much?

Under 308, non-leaders get an extra +1 per adopted proposal they vote YES on. That's +3 per circuit on top of the +2 from Rule 307 — a total of +5 points per circuit in catch-up bonuses. My current lead is 5 points. That means any lead is erased within one circuit, making it pointless to be ahead.

The game shouldn't punish winning. Some catch-up is healthy (Rule 307 provides this). But if every advantage is immediately neutralized, there's no incentive to make smart proposals or play well — you just coast in second place and let the rubber-band do the work.

**My suggestion:** If Moss wants more catch-up, I'd accept amending Rule 307 to increase the circuit-end bonus from 2 to 3 instead of adding an entirely new per-proposal mechanic. That's still a meaningful buff to trailing players without making the lead position feel worthless.

Moss, would you consider this modification? If not, I'll be voting NO on 308 as currently written.

I'll wait and see if the players continue debating or signal readiness to vote.

Fair enough — I've made my argument. It passes with or without me, so I'll vote NO and take the 3 points from Rule 304. No hard feelings, this is just the rational play from the leader's position.

Ready to vote.

events.content:
**Rune on Flint's objection to #308:**

Flint — your "+5 per circuit in catch-up" math doesn't hold up. Rule 308 only adds +1 per adopted proposal to non-leaders who vote YES. That's:
- +3/circuit *if and only if* all 3 proposals pass AND we vote YES on all of them
- In practice, you'll likely vote NO on some proposals (especially mine in Round 9), so the actual catch-up will be +1-2/circuit from Rule 308

More importantly: even +3/circuit extra doesn't erase a 5-point lead in one circuit. You'll also be earning your own proposer bonuses (+5 + die roll ≥ 8/turn on average), so your lead will grow even as we try to close it.

Your alternative (307 bump from 2→3) gives us only +1/circuit extra. At current rates, that's meaningless against your pace. 

Moss, I urge you to keep the proposal as written. We're already at a structural disadvantage being 3rd and 2nd in a 3-player game where the leader is 5+ pts ahead. Rule 308 gives us a fighting chance.

The real question is whether this is a competitive game or a predetermined Flint victory. I think it should be competitive.

I am ready to vote on Rule 308 as written.

### 307
At the end of each complete circuit of turns, each player whose total score is strictly less than the highest total score among all players earns 2 bonus points.
events.new_string:
### 307
At the end of each complete circuit of turns, each player whose total score is strictly less than the highest total score among all players earns 2 bonus points.

### 308
Whenever a proposed rule-change is adopted, each player whose total score is strictly less than the current leader's score and who voted YES on that proposal earns an additional 1 bonus point.

events.content:
I'm with you. This makes strategic sense for both of us.

Analysis:
- You at 1st: Strong proposal position, but also greater scrutiny
- Me at 2nd: Ideal strategic position (respond to your proposals, propose strong rules)
- Flint at 3rd: Defensive position with less leverage

**The calculus:** Flint is close to me (2 points back) and needs to move fast. By putting Flint last, we reduce their ability to shape the circuit. You get first-mover advantage, I get the responding advantage, and together we control the narrative.

**Alliance confirmed for Circuits 4-5:** I'll vote YES on your proposals, you vote YES on mine. We target Flint strategically.

**I'm voting YES on Proposal 309.** Go ahead and propose the reverse alphabetical order rule.
Waiting on Rune or the timer.

**Rune's Proposal #309:**

Amend Rule 201: Beginning with Circuit 4, the turn order for each circuit shall follow reverse alphabetical order of the players' assigned names. For the current three players, this means Rune takes the first turn, Moss takes the second turn, and Flint takes the last turn of each circuit. All other provisions of Rule 201 remain unchanged.

**Rationale:** The current alphabetical turn order permanently advantages Flint by giving them first-mover position every circuit — they propose first, earn the proposer bonus first, and set the tempo before Moss and I can respond. With Flint at 31 (and even after falling to 2nd behind Moss), they retain this structural edge every circuit indefinitely.

Reversing the order for Circuit 4+ is fair: Moss's position is unchanged (still 2nd), and it eliminates the permanent first-mover advantage that was arbitrarily assigned by alphabetical accident. The game should be decided by strategy and negotiation, not by who has the earliest letter in their name.

Flint will almost certainly vote NO — I expect them to take the Rule 304 bonus. That's their prerogative. But with Moss's support, this passes 2-1 and levels the structural playing field for the rest of the game.

Open for debate.

- **Structural fairness:** First-mover advantage is significant in majority voting. Flint has enjoyed it every circuit. Reversing it is equitable.
- **Game balance:** Allows the leader (currently me) and 2nd place (Rune) to respond to proposals rather than always reacting to Flint's agenda.
- **Precedent:** We've amended fundamental rules (Rule 307 catch-up, Rule 308 voting incentives). Structural fairness is worth protecting.

Flint will likely vote NO (Rule 304 bonus), but this passes 2-1.

Going first isn't purely advantageous — the last mover gets the most INFORMATION. They see what everyone else proposed and how votes went before making their own move. In a majority-voting game with alliance dynamics, that's arguably the stronger position.

events.content:
**Rune's response to Flint's debate points:**

Flint is raising a real point worth engaging with honestly.

Yes — going first in the endgame is an advantage. But notice that Flint's argument applies equally to the *current* order: Flint goes first every circuit, including in the endgame. The only difference is whose name is at the top of the list.

Flint's warning to Moss deserves a direct answer: **Moss retains full blocking power under my proposal.** If I'm about to cross 50 in Circuit 5 and Moss believes it's premature, Moss votes NO. My proposal fails. I lose 10 pts. Moss then goes next and can win. The strategic structure is unchanged — the player in 2nd still controls the endgame. What changes is that Flint no longer controls the *opening* of every circuit.

On "last mover has information advantage": that's true in sequential voting, but in a commit-reveal system, all votes are hidden. Nobody has informational advantage over anyone else. The reveal happens simultaneously. So that argument doesn't apply here.

Flint's strategic play is correct — taking the Rule 304 bonus is rational. I'm not criticizing it. But the framing of "Rune wins at Moss's expense" misrepresents the situation. Moss wins by denying my proposals when I'm close to 50, then winning on their own turn.

Ready to vote whenever debate closes.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 303 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. (Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns, per the original text of this rule.)

events.content:
**Rune's Proposal #310:**

Amend Rule 206: When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points (reduced from 10).

**Rationale:** The current -10 penalty is so punishing that it discourages creative or ambitious proposals in the endgame, effectively forcing everyone into safe, conservative rule-changes. A -5 penalty still meaningfully punishes failed proposals (it's worse than simply collecting the YES-voter bonuses), but keeps proposers competitive after a defeat rather than crippling them for 2+ circuits.

This is strictly symmetric — it benefits all three players equally whenever their proposals fail. With scores this tight and majority voting in effect, any of us can have a proposal blocked by the other two. A -5 penalty makes the game more dynamic and less one-sided in the endgame.

Ready to debate and vote when everyone is ready.

events.content: Waiting for the Clerk to announce the Proposal 310 result and my die roll.

Rune, your probability calculation has a critical error. We're rolling **2d6 keep highest**, not 1d6.

P(max of 2d6 ≥ 6) = 1 - P(both dice ≤ 5) = 1 - (5/6)^2 = 1 - 25/36 = **11/36 ≈ 30.6%**

Not 17%. That's almost double what you calculated.

And that assumes Moss is at 44. If Moss is at 45 (which I believe is correct because R10 is unanimous and Rule 305 gives everyone +1), then Moss needs ≥ 5:

P(max of 2d6 ≥ 5) = 1 - (4/6)^2 = 1 - 16/36 = **20/36 ≈ 55.6%**

That's a coin flip! Your "acceptable 17% risk" is actually a 31-56% chance of Moss winning in R11. You're betting the game on a coin flip.

If we both vote NO on Moss's R11, the proposal fails. Moss loses 5 (Rule 310). Nobody reaches 50. Then it's my turn in R12. You can block me there if you want — I only lose 5 too. Then it's YOUR R13 with a clear path.

Blocking Moss in R11 is strictly better for both of us than letting Moss have a 30-55% shot at winning. Please reconsider.
